questions.questionText: DNA replication is semi-conservative, as proven by the Meselson-Stahl experiment. The specific prediction that DISTINGUISHED semi-conservative from conservative and dispersive models was:
questions.optionA: After ONE generation in ¹⁴N, ALL DNA molecules would be INTERMEDIATE density (hybrid ¹⁵N-¹⁴N); after TWO generations, there would be 50% intermediate and 50% light — this eliminated both conservative (would show heavy+light) and dispersive (would show gradually lighter bands)
questions.optionB: All DNA would be heavy after one generation
questions.optionC: All DNA would be light after one generation
questions.explanation: Meselson-Stahl experiment (1958): E. coli grown in ¹⁵N (heavy) → transferred to ¹⁴N (light) medium. CsCl density gradient centrifugation: Gen 0: all HEAVY. Gen 1: all INTERMEDIATE (excluded conservative — which would show heavy + light). Gen 2: 50% intermediate + 50% LIGHT (excluded dispersive — which would show all bands shifting lighter uniformly). Semi-conservative: each DNA strand serves as template → daughter molecule = one old + one new strand. Key enzymes: helicase (unwinds), primase (RNA primer), DNA pol III (elongation, 5'→3'), DNA pol I (primer removal/gap fill), ligase (joins Okazaki fragments), topoisomerase (relieves supercoiling), SSB (stabilize ssDNA). Bidirectional from oriC in E. coli. Reference: Harper's — Meselson-Stahl.

questions.questionText: RNA polymerase II transcribes mRNA genes in eukaryotes. Its unique feature compared to DNA polymerase is:
questions.optionB: RNA Pol II does NOT require a primer — it initiates de novo at the promoter; it is inhibited by α-AMANITIN (Amanita mushroom toxin), recognizes promoters via TFIID (TBP binding to TATA box), and requires a CTD (C-terminal domain) for mRNA processing coupling
questions.explanation: RNA Pol II features: (1) NO PRIMER needed (unlike DNA Pol) — initiates de novo. (2) No proofreading (3'→5' exonuclease absent) → higher error rate (~10⁻⁴ vs 10⁻⁹ for DNA Pol). (3) TRANSCRIPTION INITIATION: TFIID (contains TBP/TATA-binding protein) → recognizes TATA box (~-25) → recruits TFIIA, B, F → RNA Pol II → TFIIE, H → TFIIH (helicase + kinase: phosphorylates CTD → promoter clearance). (4) α-AMANITIN sensitivity: RNA Pol I (rRNA) — resistant, RNA Pol II (mRNA) — MOST sensitive, RNA Pol III (tRNA, 5S rRNA) — intermediate. Mushroom poisoning (Amanita phalloides): α-amanitin → RNA Pol II inhibition → mRNA synthesis stops → hepatocyte death → liver failure. (5) CTD: 52 heptad repeats, phosphorylated for elongation and mRNA processing. Reference: Harper's — RNA Pol II.
questions.conceptTag: RNA Pol II amanitin CTD promoter TFIID

questions.questionText: The central dogma (DNA → RNA → protein) has important exceptions. Which of the following represents a TRUE violation of the original central dogma?
questions.optionC: Reverse transcription (RNA → DNA by reverse transcriptase in retroviruses) and RNA replication (RNA → RNA by RNA-dependent RNA polymerase in RNA viruses like influenza, hepatitis C) — both were NOT part of Crick's original 1958 formulation
questions.explanation: Central dogma — clarifications and exceptions: Crick's original (1958): information flows DNA → RNA → protein. ALLOWED transfers: DNA→DNA (replication), DNA→RNA (transcription), RNA→protein (translation). 'EXCEPTIONS' (discovered later, incorporated): (1) REVERSE TRANSCRIPTION: RNA → DNA (retrovirus: HIV RT, telomerase, LINE elements). (2) RNA → RNA: RNA-dependent RNA polymerase (RdRp) in RNA viruses (hepatitis C, influenza, SARS-CoV-2). (3) RNA DNA: rare but exists in some systems. Crick's REAL dogma (1970 revision): once information flows TO protein, it CANNOT flow back. No: protein → DNA, protein → RNA, protein → protein. Prions: controversial — protein (PrPSc) transmits structural 'information' to PrPC — but this is conformational, not sequence information. Reference: Harper's — Central Dogma.
questions.conceptTag: Central dogma exceptions reverse transcription RdRp

questions.questionText: PCR (polymerase chain reaction) amplification efficiency decreases in later cycles (plateau phase). The main reason is:
questions.optionD: Accumulation of amplicon → reannealing of product strands competes with primer annealing, pyrophosphate accumulation inhibits Taq polymerase, and substrate (dNTP) depletion occurs — together causing the plateau effect after 25-35 cycles
questions.explanation: PCR plateau phase — biochemistry: Exponential: each cycle doubles product (2ⁿ amplification). After ~25-35 cycles, efficiency drops: (1) PRODUCT REANNEALING: amplicon concentration so high that complementary product strands anneal to each other faster than primers → outcompetes primer binding. (2) PYROPHOSPHATE (PPi): released during dNTP incorporation → inhibits Taq polymerase at high concentrations. (3) dNTP DEPLETION: limiting substrate. (4) Thermal damage to Taq: though thermostable, cumulative denaturation over many cycles reduces activity. Clinical implication: explains why PCR is qualitative (end-point PCR detects presence/absence but NOT quantity). Solution: REAL-TIME/QUANTITATIVE PCR (qPCR) — measures amplification in EXPONENTIAL phase using fluorescent probes (TaqMan, SYBR) → quantitative. Ct (cycle threshold): inversely proportional to initial template. Reference: Harper's — PCR.

questions.questionText: Post-transcriptional modification of mRNA in eukaryotes includes:
questions.optionA: 5' capping (7-methylguanosine), 3' polyadenylation (poly-A tail), and splicing (intron removal)
questions.explanation: Eukaryotic mRNA processing: (1) 5' cap: 7-methylguanosine (m7G) added co-transcriptionally → protects from exonuclease, aids ribosome binding. (2) 3' poly-A tail: 100-250 adenines added by poly-A polymerase → mRNA stability, nuclear export. (3) Splicing: removal of introns (non-coding) by spliceosome (snRNPs — U1, U2, U4, U5, U6). Alternative splicing → one gene → multiple proteins. Prokaryotic mRNA: NO cap, NO poly-A, NO introns (polycistronic). Reference: Harper's — mRNA Processing.

questions.questionText: Type II restriction endonucleases are essential tools in molecular cloning. They differ from type I and type III in that type II:
questions.optionB: Cut DNA AT the specific palindromic recognition sequence itself (not away from it), require only Mg²⁺ (not ATP), and produce reproducible fragment patterns — generating blunt or sticky/cohesive ends useful for cloning
questions.explanation: Restriction endonucleases — type comparison: TYPE I: recognize specific sequence but cut RANDOMLY ~1000+ bp away. Require ATP, SAM, Mg²⁺. Restriction + modification in one complex. Not useful for cloning. TYPE II: recognize and CUT AT specific site (4-8 bp palindrome). Require only Mg²⁺. Two separate enzymes (endonuclease + methyltransferase). Examples: EcoRI (G↓AATTC, 6-bp, sticky ends), HindIII (A↓AGCTT). MOST USEFUL for cloning — predictable, reproducible cuts. Sticky (cohesive) ends: staggered cuts → 3' or 5' overhangs → can base-pair with complementary sticky ends → ligase joins. Blunt ends: cut in center → less efficient ligation. TYPE III: cut 25-27 bp from recognition site. Require ATP. Reference: Harper's — Restriction Enzymes.
questions.conceptTag: Type II restriction enzymes palindrome sticky ends

questions.questionText: Southern, Northern, and Western blots detect DNA, RNA, and protein respectively. A clinical lab uses Northern blot to detect mRNA expression. The key step that distinguishes Northern from Southern is:
questions.optionC: Northern uses DENATURED RNA (formaldehyde gel electrophoresis preserves size separation despite RNA secondary structure) with NO restriction enzyme digestion needed, while Southern requires restriction enzyme digestion of DNA before gel electrophoresis
questions.explanation: Blotting techniques — comparison: SOUTHERN (Edwin Southern, DNA): (1) DNA extracted → restriction enzyme digestion → defined fragments, (2) Agarose gel electrophoresis (separate by size), (3) Denature in situ (NaOH), (4) Transfer to membrane (nitrocellulose/nylon), (5) Hybridize with labeled DNA/RNA PROBE → detect specific DNA sequence. NORTHERN (RNA): (1) RNA extracted (no restriction digestion — RNA is already defined length = transcript size), (2) Formaldehyde/MOPS denaturing gel (denatures secondary structure), (3) Transfer + hybridize with probe → detect mRNA expression. Sizes: measures transcript size, expression level, tissue distribution. WESTERN (protein): (1) SDS-PAGE (size separation), (2) Transfer to PVDF/nitrocellulose, (3) ANTIBODY detection (primary Ab + secondary Ab-conjugate). (4) Detects: HIV (confirmatory), prion disease, etc. Eastern blot: glycoproteins/lipids (not standardized). Reference: Harper's — Blotting Techniques.
questions.conceptTag: Southern Northern Western blot comparison clinical

questions.questionText: The wobble hypothesis explains:
questions.optionD: Non-standard base pairing at the 3rd position of the codon (1st position of anticodon) → fewer tRNAs needed
questions.explanation: Wobble hypothesis (Crick): the 3rd base of the codon (5' base of anticodon) can form non-standard (wobble) base pairs. Example: inosine (I) in anticodon can pair with U, C, or A. This allows a single tRNA to recognize multiple codons coding for the same amino acid → 61 sense codons can be read by ~45 tRNAs. This explains the degeneracy of the genetic code (multiple codons for same amino acid). Reference: Harper's — Wobble.

questions.questionText: The genetic code has 64 codons for 20 amino acids + 3 stop codons. The most critical feature of degeneracy (redundancy) for clinical genetics is:
questions.optionA: Third-position (wobble) degeneracy means many SYNONYMOUS (silent) mutations at the 3rd codon position do NOT change the amino acid — providing a buffer against point mutations and explaining why not all mutations cause disease
questions.optionC: Each codon codes for multiple amino acids
questions.optionD: Stop codons can code for amino acids
questions.explanation: Genetic code degeneracy — clinical significance: 64 codons → 20 AAs + 3 stops. Degeneracy: multiple codons encode same AA (e.g., Leu: 6 codons, Ser: 6 codons). Predominantly at 3RD POSITION (wobble position): Wobble pairing (Crick): tRNA anticodon 1st position (3' end of codon) → relaxed base pairing rules → single tRNA can read multiple codons for same AA. Clinical impact: SYNONYMOUS (silent) mutations at 3rd position → usually NO amino acid change → no disease. But NOT always 'silent': can affect (1) mRNA splicing (exonic splicing enhancers), (2) mRNA stability, (3) Translation speed (codon usage bias), (4) Protein folding (rare codons → translational pausing → co-translational folding). MISSENSE: 1st or 2nd position → amino acid change → can cause disease (HbS: GAG→GTG). NONSENSE: creates stop codon → truncated protein. Reference: Harper's — Genetic Code.

questions.questionText: CRISPR-Cas9 is used for:
questions.optionB: Precise genome editing (targeted gene modification using guide RNA)
questions.explanation: CRISPR-Cas9: revolutionary genome editing tool. Guide RNA (sgRNA) directs Cas9 endonuclease to a specific DNA sequence (complementary) → Cas9 creates double-strand break → repaired by NHEJ (gene disruption/knockout) or HDR (precise gene correction/insertion). Applications: gene therapy (sickle cell — approved treatment), disease modeling, agriculture, drug development. Discovered from bacterial adaptive immune system against phages. Nobel Prize 2020 (Charpentier and Doudna). Reference: Harper's — CRISPR.

questions.questionText: Reverse transcriptase synthesizes:
questions.optionC: DNA from RNA template (RNA-dependent DNA polymerase)
questions.explanation: Reverse transcriptase (RT): RNA-dependent DNA polymerase. Found in retroviruses (HIV, HTLV). Synthesizes cDNA (complementary DNA) from RNA template. Also has RNase H activity (degrades RNA in RNA-DNA hybrid). Used in lab: cDNA library construction, RT-PCR (detect RNA viruses, measure gene expression). HIV RT: lacks proofreading (error-prone → high mutation rate → drug resistance). Anti-HIV drugs: NRTIs (zidovudine) and NNRTIs (efavirenz) target RT. Reference: Harper's — Reverse Transcriptase.

questions.questionText: Lac operon is an example of:
questions.explanation: Lac operon (E. coli, Jacob-Monod model): inducible operon — normally repressed (lac repressor binds operator → blocks transcription). When lactose present (as allolactose) → binds repressor → conformational change → repressor falls off → transcription proceeds. Maximal expression requires: allolactose (removes repressor) + cAMP-CAP (positive regulator, when glucose is LOW — catabolite repression). Trp operon: repressible (tryptophan is co-repressor). Reference: Harper's — Lac Operon.

questions.questionText: DNA damage repair by nucleotide excision repair (NER) is defective in:
questions.explanation: Xeroderma pigmentosum: AR, defective nucleotide excision repair (NER) → cannot repair UV-induced thymine dimers (pyrimidine dimers). Extreme UV sensitivity, photosensitivity, multiple skin cancers (BCC, SCC, melanoma) at young age, neurological degeneration. 7 complementation groups (XPA-XPG). NER repairs bulky lesions (UV dimers, chemical adducts). Other repair defects: HNPCC/Lynch (mismatch repair — MSH2, MLH1), Bloom syndrome (DNA helicase), Fanconi anemia (DNA crosslink repair). Reference: Harper's — XP.

questions.questionText: Eukaryotic translation initiation differs from prokaryotic in a clinically important way: eukaryotes use:
questions.optionB: 5' cap-dependent scanning mechanism — eIF4E binds m7G cap → eIF4G + eIF4A (helicase) recruit 43S pre-initiation complex → scans 5'→3' until first AUG in good Kozak context; this is why mRNA cap structure is a drug target in cancer (4E-BP/mTOR pathway)
questions.explanation: Eukaryotic translation initiation: PROKARYOTIC: 16S rRNA of 30S subunit base-pairs with Shine-Dalgarno sequence (AGGAGG, ~7bp upstream of AUG) → direct AUG recognition. Uses fMet-tRNA. 70S ribosomes. EUKARYOTIC: (1) eIF4E recognizes 5' m7G CAP, (2) eIF4G (scaffold) + eIF4A (RNA helicase) = eIF4F complex, (3) Recruits 43S PIC (40S + eIF1,1A,2,3,5 + Met-tRNAi), (4) SCANNING: 43S scans 5'→3' through 5'UTR until first AUG in KOZAK consensus (gcc(A/G)ccAUGG), (5) 60S joins → 80S ribosome → elongation. Uses Met-tRNAi (NOT fMet). Clinical relevance: mTORC1 phosphorylates 4E-BP → releases eIF4E → translation ON. mTOR inhibitors (rapamycin, everolimus): block → translation OFF → cancer therapy. IRES (internal ribosome entry site): cap-independent, used by HCV, poliovirus. Reference: Harper's — Eukaryotic Translation Initiation.
questions.conceptTag: Eukaryotic cap scanning Kozak mTOR eIF4E

questions.questionText: Proto-oncogenes become oncogenes by:
questions.optionC: Gain-of-function mutations (point mutations, amplification, translocation → constitutive activation)
questions.explanation: Proto-oncogenes → oncogenes: gain-of-function (dominant — one allele sufficient). Mechanisms: (1) Point mutation (RAS — most commonly mutated oncogene, constitutively active GTPase). (2) Gene amplification (HER2/ERBB2 in breast cancer, N-MYC in neuroblastoma). (3) Chromosomal translocation (BCR-ABL t(9;22) in CML — Philadelphia chromosome; c-MYC t(8;14) in Burkitt lymphoma). Tumor suppressors: loss-of-function (both alleles — two-hit hypothesis, Knudson). Reference: Harper's — Oncogenes.

questions.questionText: Which DNA repair pathway primarily removes bulky thymine dimers induced by UV radiation?
questions.explanation: Correct answer: D. Nucleotide excision repair removes bulky DNA adducts like pyrimidine dimers; defects cause xeroderma pigmentosum with photosensitivity and skin cancer risk.

questions.questionText: Southern blot is the gold standard confirmatory test for certain genetic conditions. In clinical practice, the specific advantage of Southern blot for diagnosing trinucleotide repeat disorders (like fragile X) over PCR is:
questions.optionA: Very large expansions (>200 CGG repeats in FMR1 for fragile X, >1000 bp) CANNOT be amplified by standard PCR due to extreme GC content and repeat length — Southern blot using methylation-sensitive restriction enzymes can detect both expansion size AND methylation status simultaneously
questions.explanation: Southern blot in fragile X diagnosis: Fragile X syndrome: FMR1 gene on Xq27.3. Normal: 5-44 CGG repeats. Premutation: 55-200 (carrier, risk of FXTAS/FXPOI). Full mutation: >200 → CpG METHYLATION → FMR1 silencing → no FMRP → intellectual disability. PCR limitation: standard PCR CANNOT amplify >~200 CGG repeats (extremely GC-rich → secondary structures → polymerase stalling). Triplet-repeat primed PCR can detect expansions but not size accurately. Southern blot advantages: (1) No size limit → detects any expansion, (2) Methylation-sensitive restriction enzymes (EagI combined with EcoRI): can determine METHYLATION STATUS (methylated = full mutation with gene silencing vs unmethylated = premutation/normal). (3) Distinguishes mosaic patterns. Gold standard for fragile X diagnosis. Reference: Harper's — Fragile X Southern Blot.
questions.conceptTag: Southern blot fragile X trinucleotide methylation

questions.questionText: In prokaryotes, the lac operon is induced in the presence of lactose when the repressor:
questions.explanation: Correct answer: B. Allolactose (inducer) binds lac repressor and reduces operator binding, permitting transcription. CAP-cAMP further enhances expression when glucose is low.

questions.questionText: CRISPR-Cas9 gene editing technology uses which of the following to target specific DNA sequences?
questions.optionC: A guide RNA (gRNA) complementary to the target DNA sequence directs Cas9 endonuclease to create double-strand breaks
questions.explanation: CRISPR-Cas9: revolutionary gene editing tool. Components: (1) Cas9 endonuclease (molecular scissors — creates DSBs), (2) guide RNA (gRNA/sgRNA — 20-nucleotide sequence complementary to target DNA, directs Cas9 to specific genomic location). After DSB: (1) NHEJ (non-homologous end joining — error-prone, gene knockout), (2) HDR (homology-directed repair — precise, gene correction using template DNA). Applications: gene therapy for sickle cell disease, β-thalassemia, cancer immunotherapy. Nobel Prize in Chemistry 2020 (Doudna & Charpentier). Reference: Harper's — CRISPR Technology.

questions.questionText: Mismatch repair (MMR) deficiency is associated with:
questions.optionD: Hereditary nonpolyposis colorectal cancer (HNPCC/Lynch syndrome — microsatellite instability)
questions.explanation: Mismatch repair (MMR): corrects base mismatches and insertion/deletion loops that escape proofreading. Key genes: MLH1, MSH2, MSH6, PMS2. Deficiency → microsatellite instability (MSI) — microsatellites are repetitive DNA sequences that become unstable with MMR loss. Lynch syndrome (HNPCC): autosomal dominant, germline MMR gene mutations → increased risk of colorectal, endometrial, ovarian cancers. MSI-high tumors: respond well to immune checkpoint inhibitors (pembrolizumab). Prokaryotic MMR: MutS (recognizes mismatch), MutL, MutH (nicks unmethylated strand). Reference: Harper's — Mismatch Repair.

questions.questionText: Nucleotide excision repair (NER) is defective in:
questions.optionA: Xeroderma pigmentosum (UV-induced pyrimidine dimers cannot be repaired)
questions.explanation: NER: repairs bulky, helix-distorting DNA lesions (UV-induced thymine dimers, chemical adducts, crosslinks). Steps: damage recognition → dual incision (5' and 3' to lesion, removing ~24-32 nt oligonucleotide) → gap filling by DNA polymerase → ligation. Xeroderma pigmentosum (XP): autosomal recessive, defective NER → extreme UV sensitivity, >1000x increased skin cancer risk (basal cell, squamous cell, melanoma), neurological degeneration. 7 complementation groups (XPA-XPG). BER (base excision repair): repairs small base modifications (deamination, oxidation, alkylation) — uses DNA glycosylases. Reference: Harper's — NER and XP.

questions.questionText: Telomerase is a specialized enzyme that:
questions.optionB: Extends telomeric DNA (TTAGGG repeats) using its built-in RNA template — a reverse transcriptase
questions.explanation: Telomerase: ribonucleoprotein enzyme with reverse transcriptase activity (TERT subunit) and integral RNA template (TERC — contains complementary sequence to telomeric repeat TTAGGG). Solves the 'end-replication problem' — conventional DNA polymerase cannot fully replicate 3' ends of linear chromosomes. Active in: germ cells, stem cells, most cancer cells (>85%). Absent/low in most somatic cells → progressive telomere shortening with each division → replicative senescence (Hayflick limit). Cancer: telomerase reactivation enables unlimited proliferation (immortalization). Reference: Harper's — Telomerase.

questions.questionText: The Shine-Dalgarno sequence is important in:
questions.optionC: Prokaryotic translation initiation (ribosome binding site on mRNA — complementary to 16S rRNA 3' end)
questions.explanation: Shine-Dalgarno (SD) sequence: purine-rich sequence (typically AGGAGG) located 5-10 nucleotides upstream of the AUG start codon in PROKARYOTIC mRNA. Complementary to the 3' end of 16S rRNA in the 30S ribosomal subunit → positions the ribosome correctly for translation initiation at the AUG start codon. ABSENT in eukaryotic mRNAs — eukaryotes use Kozak sequence (5'-GCCACCAUGG-3') and 5' cap recognition for ribosome binding (scanning mechanism). Reference: Harper's — Prokaryotic Translation Initiation.

questions.questionText: Epigenetic modification that typically silences gene expression is:
questions.optionD: DNA methylation at CpG islands (by DNA methyltransferases — DNMT)
questions.explanation: DNA methylation: addition of methyl group to cytosine at CpG dinucleotides (by DNMTs using SAM as methyl donor). CpG islands: CG-rich regions in gene promoters. Methylated CpG → gene silencing (recruits methyl-CpG binding proteins → attract HDACs → chromatin condensation). Unmethylated → gene active. Epigenetic = heritable changes in gene expression WITHOUT DNA sequence changes. Histone modifications: Acetylation (by HATs) → gene activation (open chromatin/euchromatin). Deacetylation (by HDACs) → gene silencing (heterochromatin). Cancer: abnormal methylation patterns (hypermethylation of tumor suppressors, global hypomethylation). Reference: Harper's — Epigenetics.

questions.questionText: MicroRNAs (miRNAs) regulate gene expression by:
questions.optionA: Binding to the 3' UTR of target mRNAs → translational repression or mRNA degradation (post-transcriptional silencing)
questions.explanation: miRNAs: small (~22 nucleotide) non-coding RNAs that regulate gene expression post-transcriptionally. Mechanism: pri-miRNA → pre-miRNA (Drosha in nucleus) → mature miRNA (Dicer in cytoplasm) → loaded into RISC (RNA-induced silencing complex) → binds complementary sequence in 3' UTR of target mRNA → translational inhibition and/or mRNA degradation. Single miRNA can regulate hundreds of target genes. Involved in: development, differentiation, apoptosis, proliferation. Dysregulated in cancer (oncomiRs, tumor suppressor miRNAs). Circulating miRNAs: potential diagnostic biomarkers. Reference: Harper's — miRNA Gene Regulation.

questions.questionText: In eukaryotic mRNA processing, the 5' cap structure consists of:
questions.optionB: 7-methylguanosine linked via 5'-5' triphosphate bridge to the first nucleotide
questions.explanation: 5' cap: added co-transcriptionally to pre-mRNA. Structure: 7-methylguanosine (m7G) attached via unusual 5'-5' triphosphate bond. Functions: (1) Protection from 5' exonuclease degradation, (2) mRNA stability, (3) Nuclear export (recognized by CBC — cap-binding complex), (4) Translation initiation (recognized by eIF4E → recruits ribosome). Cap added by: guanylyltransferase (adds GMP), methyltransferases (methylate guanine N7 and ribose 2'-O). 3' processing: polyadenylation signal (AAUAAA) → cleavage → poly-A tail (~200 adenines) added by poly-A polymerase. Reference: Harper's — mRNA Capping.

questions.questionText: In CRISPR-Cas9 gene editing, the guide RNA (sgRNA) has two critical components. The specificity of CRISPR targeting depends on:
questions.optionC: A 20-nucleotide SPACER sequence in the sgRNA that base-pairs with the target DNA + a PAM sequence (5'-NGG-3' for SpCas9) adjacent to the target on the genomic DNA — both are required for Cas9-mediated double-strand break
questions.explanation: CRISPR-Cas9 mechanism: sgRNA (single guide RNA): contains (1) 20-nt SPACER (complementary to target DNA — provides specificity), (2) tracrRNA scaffold (binds Cas9). Targeting requires: (1) Spacer-target complementarity, (2) PAM (protospacer adjacent motif): 5'-NGG-3' (SpCas9) on the NON-TARGET strand immediately 3' to the target. PAM is essential — Cas9 first scans for PAM, then unwinds DNA to check spacer complementarity. DSB: Cas9 has 2 nuclease domains (RuvC cuts non-target strand, HNH cuts target strand) → blunt DSB 3bp upstream of PAM. Repair: (1) NHEJ (error-prone) → insertions/deletions → gene knockout, (2) HDR (with template) → precise editing. Off-target: spacer mismatches can cause unwanted cuts → improved Cas9 variants (eSpCas9, HiFi). Applications: gene therapy (sickle cell, thalassemia), crop improvement, disease models. Reference: Harper's — CRISPR.
questions.conceptTag: CRISPR sgRNA spacer PAM Cas9 DSB mechanism

questions.questionText: Real-time quantitative PCR (qPCR) differs from conventional end-point PCR in a clinically critical way:
questions.optionA: qPCR does not use Taq polymerase
questions.optionD: qPCR measures amplification in REAL-TIME during the EXPONENTIAL phase using fluorescent detection (TaqMan probes or SYBR Green), enabling QUANTIFICATION of initial template — essential for viral load monitoring (HIV, HCV, SARS-CoV-2), minimal residual disease, and gene expression analysis
questions.explanation: qPCR — quantitative molecular diagnostics: Conventional PCR: end-point detection (gel electrophoresis after amplification complete → plateau) → qualitative (present/absent) only. qPCR: fluorescence measured EVERY CYCLE → amplification curve plotted in real-time. Detection methods: (1) SYBR Green: intercalates dsDNA → fluoresces. Non-specific (any dsDNA). Cheap. Melting curve for specificity. (2) TaqMan: probe with fluorophore + quencher hybridizes between primers → Taq 5'→3' exonuclease cleaves probe → fluorophore released → signal. SPECIFIC. Ct (cycle threshold): cycle at which fluorescence crosses threshold. Ct is INVERSELY proportional to log(initial template). Lower Ct = more template. Clinical uses: (1) HIV viral load (copies/mL), (2) HCV RNA quantification (treatment monitoring), (3) SARS-CoV-2 diagnosis (Ct<30 typically infectious), (4) BCR-ABL monitoring in CML (minimal residual disease). Reference: Harper's — qPCR.
questions.conceptTag: qPCR TaqMan SYBR Ct viral load quantification

questions.questionText: Western blot is the confirmatory test for HIV diagnosis. The principle involves:
questions.optionA: HIV proteins separated by SDS-PAGE → transferred to membrane → patient's serum applied → if HIV antibodies present, they bind specific viral proteins (gp160, gp120, gp41, p24, p31); at least 2 of 3 bands (p24, gp41, gp160/120) must be present for positive result
questions.explanation: Western blot for HIV: Procedure: (1) HIV viral proteins separated by SDS-PAGE (molecular weight), (2) Transferred (blotted) to nitrocellulose membrane, (3) Patient's serum incubated with membrane, (4) If anti-HIV antibodies present → bind specific viral protein bands, (5) Detected by enzyme-conjugated anti-human IgG secondary antibody + substrate → band visualization. Interpretation (CDC criteria): POSITIVE: at least 2 of 3 bands: p24 (gag capsid), gp41 (env transmembrane), gp120/160 (env surface). NEGATIVE: no bands. INDETERMINATE: some bands but doesn't meet positive criteria (may be seroconversion → repeat in 4-6 weeks). HIV-1 vs HIV-2: different band patterns. Now largely replaced by 4th generation ELISA (detects both Ab + p24 Ag) + HIV-1/HIV-2 differentiation assay. PCR: used for neonatal diagnosis (maternal Ab transferred → serology unreliable <18 months). Reference: Harper's — HIV Western Blot.
questions.conceptTag: Western blot HIV confirmation bands p24 gp41

questions.questionText: RNA splicing removes introns from pre-mRNA. This is catalyzed by:
questions.optionB: Spliceosome (complex of snRNPs — U1, U2, U4, U5, U6 — and associated proteins)
questions.explanation: Spliceosome: large ribonucleoprotein complex that catalyzes removal of introns from pre-mRNA. Composed of 5 small nuclear RNAs (snRNAs: U1, U2, U4, U5, U6) complexed with proteins → snRNPs ('snurps'). Mechanism: U1 recognizes 5' splice site (GU), U2 recognizes branch point (adenine), catalytic steps involve transesterification reactions → lariat intermediate formed → intron excised as lariat → exons joined. Alternative splicing: same gene → different mRNAs → different proteins (increases proteome diversity). Group I and II introns: self-splicing ribozymes (no spliceosome needed). Reference: Harper's — RNA Splicing.

questions.questionText: Okazaki fragments are formed on the lagging strand during DNA replication. The clinical significance of defective Okazaki fragment processing is seen in:
questions.optionC: Defective FEN1 (flap endonuclease 1) or DNA ligase I → unjoined Okazaki fragments → single-strand breaks accumulate → genome instability; DNA ligase I deficiency causes immunodeficiency with growth retardation, and FEN1 mutations are linked to cancer predisposition
questions.explanation: Okazaki fragment processing — clinical relevance: Lagging strand: synthesized as 100-200 nt Okazaki fragments (eukaryotes), each initiated by RNA primer (primase/Pol α). Processing: (1) DNA Pol δ extends → encounters downstream primer, (2) Strand displacement creates a 5' FLAP of displaced RNA/DNA, (3) FEN1 (flap endonuclease 1): cleaves 5' flap, (4) DNA ligase I: seals nick → continuous strand. Defects: DNA LIGASE I deficiency: autosomal recessive — immunodeficiency (impaired V(D)J recombination and class switch), photosensitivity, growth retardation. FEN1: haploinsufficiency → mutations accumulate → cancer predisposition (lung, breast, GI). DNA LIGASE IV deficiency (not Okazaki-related but NHEJ): LIG4 syndrome — microcephaly, immunodeficiency, radiosensitivity. Compare: Bloom syndrome (BLM helicase) → sister chromatid exchanges. Reference: Harper's — Okazaki Fragment Processing.
questions.conceptTag: Okazaki FEN1 ligase I deficiency genome instability

questions.questionText: Which RNA polymerase transcribes mRNA (protein-coding genes) in eukaryotes?
questions.optionD: RNA Polymerase II (most sensitive to α-amanitin — mushroom toxin)
questions.explanation: Eukaryotic RNA polymerases: RNA Pol I (nucleolus): transcribes rRNA genes (45S pre-rRNA → 28S, 18S, 5.8S rRNA). Resistant to α-amanitin. RNA Pol II (nucleoplasm): transcribes mRNA, snRNA, miRNA. Most sensitive to α-amanitin (Amanita phalloides mushroom toxin — causes fatal hepatorenal failure). Requires TFIID (recognizes TATA box via TBP), TFIIH (helicase + kinase — phosphorylates CTD of Pol II). RNA Pol III: transcribes tRNA, 5S rRNA, snRNA. Intermediate α-amanitin sensitivity. Prokaryotes: single RNA polymerase (core enzyme: α2ββ'ω + σ factor). Reference: Harper's — RNA Polymerases.

questions.questionText: Trinucleotide repeat expansion causing anticipation is seen in:
questions.optionA: Huntington disease (CAG repeat expansion in HTT gene → polyglutamine tract expansion)
questions.explanation: Trinucleotide repeat disorders: unstable repeats that expand across generations → increasing disease severity/earlier onset (ANTICIPATION). CAG repeat (polyglutamine): Huntington disease (HTT gene, >36 repeats), spinocerebellar ataxias, SBMA (Kennedy disease). CGG repeat: Fragile X syndrome (FMR1 gene, >200 full mutation). CTG repeat: myotonic dystrophy (DMPK gene). GAA repeat: Friedreich ataxia (FXN gene — only autosomal RECESSIVE repeat disorder). Normal range varies by gene. Repeat length correlates with: severity, age of onset, tendency for further expansion (especially paternal transmission for CAG). Reference: Harper's — Trinucleotide Repeat Disorders.

questions.questionText: Proto-oncogenes become oncogenes through:
questions.optionB: Gain-of-function mechanisms (point mutation, amplification, translocation, viral insertion → constitutive activation)
questions.explanation: Proto-oncogene → oncogene conversion (gain of function): (1) Point mutation: Ras (codon 12/13/61 — constitutively active GTPase), (2) Gene amplification: MYC (Burkitt lymphoma), HER2/ERBB2 (breast cancer), (3) Chromosomal translocation: BCR-ABL t(9;22) Philadelphia chromosome (CML), MYC t(8;14) (Burkitt), (4) Viral insertion: v-src, v-myc. Tumor suppressors (Rb, p53, APC, BRCA): LOSS of function (need 'two hits' — Knudson hypothesis). Oncogene activation needs only ONE allele affected (dominant effect). Proto-oncogenes: normal genes involved in growth, proliferation, survival. Reference: Harper's — Oncogenes.

questions.questionText: Next-generation sequencing (NGS) differs from Sanger sequencing in that NGS:
questions.optionA: Can only sequence one gene at a time
questions.optionC: Performs massively parallel sequencing of millions of DNA fragments simultaneously → whole genome/exome sequencing possible
questions.explanation: NGS (next-generation/high-throughput sequencing): massively parallel approach — millions of DNA fragments sequenced simultaneously, then computationally assembled. Platforms: Illumina (sequencing by synthesis), Ion Torrent, PacBio (long reads), Oxford Nanopore. Applications: whole genome sequencing (WGS), whole exome sequencing (WES — coding regions only), RNA-seq, clinical diagnostics, pharmacogenomics, tumor profiling. Sanger sequencing: chain termination method using ddNTPs (gold standard for single gene/mutation verification but low throughput). Reference: Harper's — NGS Technology.

questions.questionText: Base excision repair (BER) corrects DNA damage caused by:
questions.optionD: Spontaneous deamination, oxidation, and alkylation of bases (small, non-helix-distorting lesions)
questions.explanation: BER: repairs small base modifications that don't significantly distort DNA helix. Steps: (1) DNA glycosylase recognizes and removes damaged base (cleaves N-glycosidic bond → creates AP/apurinic-apyrimidinic site), (2) AP endonuclease nicks backbone, (3) DNA polymerase β fills gap, (4) DNA ligase seals. Common lesions repaired: deaminated cytosine (uracil — recognized by uracil-DNA glycosylase/UDG), oxidized guanine (8-oxoguanine — by OGG1), alkylated bases. Each type has specific glycosylase. NER handles bulky lesions; MMR handles mismatches. Reference: Harper's — Base Excision Repair.

questions.questionText: siRNA (small interfering RNA) differs from miRNA (microRNA) primarily in that:
questions.optionA: siRNA is perfectly complementary to a SINGLE target mRNA causing degradation, while miRNA has imperfect complementarity and can regulate MULTIPLE target mRNAs by translational repression
questions.optionB: siRNA is single-stranded while miRNA is double-stranded
questions.optionC: miRNA is artificially synthesized while siRNA is natural
questions.optionD: siRNA acts in the nucleus while miRNA acts in the cytoplasm
questions.explanation: siRNA vs miRNA: BOTH are ~20-25 nt small RNAs functioning through RISC. siRNA: perfectly complementary to ONE mRNA → mRNA CLEAVAGE. Therapeutic siRNAs: patisiran (TTR amyloidosis), givosiran (AIP). miRNA: endogenous, imperfect complementarity → binds 3'-UTR of MULTIPLE mRNAs → translational REPRESSION. One miRNA can regulate ~200 targets. Dysregulation in cancer: oncomiRs (miR-21), tumor suppressor miRNAs (let-7, miR-34). Reference: Harper's — siRNA vs miRNA.

questions.questionText: Adeno-associated virus (AAV) is the preferred vector for MOST gene therapy applications because:
questions.optionA: It integrates into the host genome ensuring permanent expression
questions.optionB: AAV is non-pathogenic, causes minimal immune response, provides long-term expression in non-dividing cells WITHOUT genomic integration (episomal persistence), and multiple serotypes allow tissue targeting
questions.optionC: It can carry unlimited DNA insert sizes
questions.optionD: It replicates autonomously in host cells
questions.explanation: AAV gene therapy vector: Non-pathogenic, minimal immunogenicity, long-term expression in post-mitotic cells as episome (low insertional mutagenesis risk). Multiple serotypes for tissue targeting (AAV9 crosses BBB, AAV8 for liver). Limitation: small insert (~4.7 kb). FDA-approved: Luxturna (RPE65), Zolgensma (SMN1/SMA), Hemgenix (FIX/hemophilia B). Lentiviral vectors integrate — used for ex vivo HSC gene therapy. Reference: Harper's — AAV Gene Therapy.

questions.questionText: Liquid biopsy detects circulating tumor DNA (ctDNA) in blood. The primary advantage over tissue biopsy is:
questions.optionB: It only works for hematological malignancies
questions.optionC: It is non-invasive, captures tumor heterogeneity from multiple sites, enables real-time monitoring of treatment response and resistance mutations, and can detect minimal residual disease
questions.optionD: It provides higher DNA quality than tissue
questions.explanation: Liquid biopsy/ctDNA: Non-invasive (blood draw), captures SPATIAL heterogeneity (all tumor sites), TEMPORAL monitoring (serial sampling shows resistance mutations, e.g., EGFR T790M), MRD detection. Detection: digital PCR, NGS. FDA-approved: Guardant360 CDx, FoundationOne Liquid CDx. Limitation: low ctDNA in early-stage disease. Reference: Harper's — Liquid Biopsy.

questions.questionText: DNA methylation at CpG islands in gene promoters generally leads to:
questions.optionD: Gene SILENCING — methyl groups recruit MBD proteins that attract HDACs → heterochromatin formation
questions.explanation: DNA methylation silencing: DNMTs (DNMT1 maintenance, DNMT3A/3B de novo) use SAM as methyl donor. Methylated CpGs → recruit MeCP2 → HDAC complexes → histone deacetylation → chromatin compaction → silencing. Cancer: global HYPOmethylation (genomic instability) + local HYPERmethylation of tumor suppressor promoters (e.g., MGMT silencing → better temozolomide response; p16 silencing). 5-azacitidine/decitabine: DNMT inhibitors → reactivate silenced genes. Reference: Harper's — DNA Methylation.
questions.conceptTag: DNA methylation CpG gene silencing cancer

questions.questionText: Iron regulatory proteins (IRPs) control iron homeostasis at the translational level. When intracellular iron is LOW, IRP binding to IREs results in:
questions.optionA: DECREASED ferritin translation (IRP binds 5'-UTR IRE blocking ribosome) AND INCREASED transferrin receptor mRNA stability (IRP binds 3'-UTR IREs protecting from degradation)
questions.optionB: Increased ferritin synthesis and decreased transferrin receptor
questions.explanation: IRE/IRP system: LOW iron → IRP active → (1) 5'-UTR IRE (ferritin mRNA): blocks ribosome → ferritin DECREASED, (2) 3'-UTR IREs (TfR mRNA): protects from endonuclease → TfR INCREASED. HIGH iron → IRP inactive → ferritin translated (store iron), TfR degraded (stop uptake). Elegant post-transcriptional regulation. Reference: Harper's — IRE/IRP System.

questions.questionText: Antisense oligonucleotides (ASOs) work therapeutically by:
questions.optionB: Binding target pre-mRNA/mRNA to modulate splicing (exon skipping/inclusion) or trigger RNase H-mediated mRNA degradation
questions.optionC: Activating immune cells to attack disease cells
questions.explanation: ASOs: 15-25 nt single-stranded synthetic nucleic acids. Two mechanisms: (1) RNase H-dependent: ASO:mRNA hybrid → RNase H cleaves mRNA → knockdown (mipomersen → ApoB mRNA). (2) Splice-switching: nusinersen (SMA — promotes SMN2 exon 7 inclusion), eteplirsen (DMD — exon 51 skipping restores dystrophin reading frame). Chemical modifications: phosphorothioate, 2'-O-methyl, LNA. Reference: Harper's — ASO Therapeutics.

questions.questionText: Histone acetylation by HATs promotes gene transcription because:
questions.optionA: It adds methyl groups to DNA
questions.optionC: Acetylation neutralizes positive charge on lysine residues → weakens histone-DNA interaction → open chromatin (euchromatin) → transcription factor access
questions.explanation: HATs: acetyl-CoA → acetyl group on lysine ε-amino → neutralize positive charge → weaker electrostatic interaction with negative DNA → OPEN chromatin → euchromatin → genes accessible. HDACs: remove acetyl → compact chromatin → silencing. HDAC inhibitors (vorinostat, romidepsin): anticancer — reactivate tumor suppressors. Approved for CTCL. Histone code: acetylation (activation), methylation (activation OR repression by residue). Reference: Harper's — Histone Acetylation.

questions.questionText: RNA editing by ADAR converts adenosine to inosine. The most clinically relevant example is:
questions.optionD: GluR-B mRNA Q/R site editing — converts glutamine to arginine codon, critically controlling calcium permeability of AMPA receptors
questions.explanation: ADAR: A→I editing (inosine read as G by ribosome → A-to-G change). GluR-B (GRIA2): unedited = Ca2+-permeable AMPA receptor; edited (>99%) = Ca2+-impermeable. Failure → excitotoxicity → linked to ALS, epilepsy, glioblastoma. Also: APOBEC1 edits ApoB mRNA (C→U) in intestine: CAA→UAA stop → ApoB-48 for chylomicrons (liver: full ApoB-100 for VLDL). Reference: Harper's — RNA Editing.

questions.questionText: Trinucleotide repeat expansion disorders share which molecular feature?
questions.optionA: Unstable expansion of repeats in successive generations with ANTICIPATION — earlier onset and greater severity each generation
questions.explanation: Trinucleotide repeats expand by slipped-strand mispairing. ANTICIPATION: repeat number increases each generation → earlier/worse disease. Huntington: CAG in HTT (>36 repeats, polyglutamine). Fragile X: CGG in FMR1 (>200 → methylation → silencing). Myotonic dystrophy: CTG in DMPK (toxic RNA). Friedrich ataxia: GAA intronic (loss of frataxin). Reference: Harper's — Trinucleotide Repeats.

questions.questionText: Next-generation sequencing (NGS) differs from Sanger sequencing fundamentally because:
questions.optionA: NGS uses radioactive labels while Sanger uses fluorescence
questions.optionB: NGS performs massively parallel sequencing of millions of fragments simultaneously; Sanger sequences one fragment at a time
questions.optionC: Sanger is more accurate for whole genome analysis
questions.explanation: NGS: massively parallel (millions-billions fragments simultaneously). Platforms: Illumina (sequencing by synthesis), PacBio (long-read), Nanopore (real-time). Applications: WGS, WES (~1.5% genome, ~85% disease mutations), RNA-seq, cancer panels. Cost drop: $3B (Human Genome Project) → ~$200 (2024). Sanger: gold standard accuracy but one fragment at a time. Reference: Harper's — NGS Technology.

questions.questionText: The mTOR pathway regulates translation by phosphorylating:
questions.optionC: 4E-BP1 (releasing eIF4E for cap-dependent translation) and S6K1 (ribosomal protein S6 for ribosome biogenesis)
questions.explanation: mTORC1 (rapamycin-sensitive): activated by growth factors, amino acids (leucine), ATP, insulin. Targets: (1) 4E-BP1: phosphorylated → releases eIF4E → cap-dependent translation initiated. (2) S6K1: phosphorylates S6 → ribosome biogenesis. Rapamycin/sirolimus: mTORC1 inhibitor → immunosuppressant, antiproliferative. Everolimus/temsirolimus: anticancer. Reference: Harper's — mTOR Signaling.

questions.questionText: Which of the following is NOT an epigenetic mechanism?
questions.optionD: Point mutation (G→A) in a coding region — this changes DNA sequence and is a GENETIC alteration
questions.explanation: EPIGENETIC: heritable gene expression changes WITHOUT DNA sequence change — DNA methylation, histone modifications, non-coding RNA, chromatin remodeling. Potentially REVERSIBLE (DNMT inhibitors, HDAC inhibitors). GENETIC: changes IN DNA sequence (mutations). Point mutation = GENETIC. Epigenetics explains X-inactivation, genomic imprinting, twin differences. Reference: Harper's — Epigenetics.

questions.questionText: A DNA fragment is hydrolyzed and yields adenine, deoxyribose, and phosphate in one product unit. This unit is best identified as:
questions.explanation: Correct answer: a nucleotide contains base + sugar + phosphate, whereas a nucleoside is base + sugar only. Option A lacks phosphate. Option B is incomplete because it has no sugar-phosphate. Option D belongs to membrane lipid chemistry, not nucleic acids. Key trap: nucleotide versus nucleoside naming.

questions.questionText: All are correct regarding DNA nucleotide chemistry EXCEPT:
questions.optionA: N-glycosidic bond links phosphate to 3' carbon of deoxyribose
questions.optionC: Pyrimidines are cytosine and thymine in DNA
questions.optionD: Phosphodiester linkage joins 3' OH of one nucleotide to 5' phosphate of the next
questions.explanation: Correct answer (EXCEPT): N-glycosidic bond links base to 1' carbon of sugar, not phosphate to 3' carbon. Options B and C correctly classify purines/pyrimidines. Option D correctly defines the 3'-5' phosphodiester backbone. Key differentiating clue: glycosidic is base-sugar; phosphodiester is sugar-phosphate-sugar.

questions.questionText: Which statement is INCORRECT about A, B, and Z DNA?
questions.optionA: B-DNA is right-handed and is the predominant physiologic form
questions.optionB: A-DNA is right-handed, shorter and wider than B-DNA
questions.optionC: Z-DNA is left-handed and favored by alternating purine-pyrimidine sequences
questions.optionD: Z-DNA has a broad major groove that is the usual protein-binding groove in genomic DNA
questions.explanation: Correct answer: Z-DNA is left-handed with zig-zag backbone and is not the usual broad-major-groove protein-binding form for routine genomic interactions. A and B describe canonical B and A forms correctly. C is correct for Z-DNA preference. Key trap: mixing groove characteristics across helix forms.
questions.conceptTag: A B Z DNA comparison trap

questions.questionText: Which change most strongly increases DNA melting temperature (Tm) for a fragment of similar length and salt conditions?
questions.explanation: Correct answer: GC-rich DNA has higher Tm because GC pairs contribute greater duplex stability than AT pairs. Option A lowers Tm. Options C and D destabilize duplex and reduce effective melting behavior. Clinical relevance: primer design and hybridization stringency in PCR/blotting depend on Tm.

questions.questionText: Regarding DNA grooves, the best exam-relevant statement is:
questions.optionA: Minor groove universally provides the richest sequence-specific pattern for transcription factors
questions.optionC: Major groove in B-DNA exposes a richer base-specific chemical pattern for many sequence-specific DNA-binding proteins
questions.optionD: Groove geometry has no role in protein-DNA recognition
questions.explanation: Correct answer: B-DNA major groove exposes discriminative hydrogen-bond donor/acceptor patterns, aiding sequence-specific recognition. Option A is overgeneralized and often false for canonical transcription factors. Option B is false because all helical forms have grooves. Option D ignores core structural biology of DNA-protein binding.

questions.questionText: A patient with gram-negative sepsis is treated with a quinolone that inhibits bacterial DNA gyrase. The most direct replication consequence is:
questions.optionA: Failure to relieve positive supercoils ahead of the replication fork
questions.explanation: Correct answer: DNA gyrase (bacterial type II topoisomerase) introduces negative supercoils and relieves torsional stress ahead of forks. Option B is unrelated eukaryotic enzyme targeting. Option C is transcriptional RNA processing, not DNA topology. Option D is translation-level and unrelated. Key trap: topoisomerase function versus polymerase function.

questions.questionText: Which paired statement is most accurate?
questions.optionA: DNA polymerase synthesizes 3' to 5' and can initiate de novo
questions.optionB: RNA polymerase requires an RNA primer but DNA polymerase does not
questions.optionC: Both DNA and RNA polymerases require preexisting 3' OH primers
questions.optionD: DNA polymerase extends only 5' to 3' from a primer 3' OH, whereas RNA polymerase initiates RNA de novo at promoters
questions.explanation: Correct answer: DNA polymerases extend 5' to 3' and require a primer 3' OH; RNA polymerases initiate transcription without primer. Option A reverses both rules. Option B swaps requirements incorrectly. Option C falsely assigns primer dependence to RNA polymerase. Mechanistic clue: chemistry of nucleophilic attack requires 3' OH extension.
questions.conceptTag: Primer requirement DNA versus RNA polymerase

questions.questionText: Which comparison best distinguishes prokaryotic from eukaryotic DNA replication?
questions.optionA: Eukaryotes typically have one origin per chromosome and prokaryotes have thousands
questions.optionB: Eukaryotes have multiple origins per linear chromosome and shorter Okazaki fragments than prokaryotes
questions.optionC: Prokaryotes require telomerase for chromosome end replication
questions.optionD: Only eukaryotes show bidirectional replication forks
questions.explanation: Correct answer: eukaryotic chromosomes use multiple origins and typically shorter Okazaki fragments (about 100-200 nt) than prokaryotes (about 1000-2000 nt). Option A is reversed. Option C is false because bacterial chromosomes are usually circular and do not face classic end-replication problem. Option D is false; replication is bidirectional in both systems.

questions.questionText: In prokaryotic transcription, rho-dependent termination requires:
questions.optionA: Rho ATP-dependent helicase binding rut sites and dissociating RNA-DNA hybrid at paused polymerase
questions.optionB: GC-rich hairpin followed by poly-U tract only
questions.explanation: Correct answer: rho-dependent termination uses rho helicase, ATP, and rut-site interaction to release transcript. Option B describes rho-independent (intrinsic) termination. Option C is eukaryotic RNA processing. Option D is eukaryotic 3' end processing, not bacterial termination. Key trap: intrinsic terminator versus rho factor.

questions.questionText: All are true regarding α-amanitin sensitivity EXCEPT:
questions.optionA: RNA polymerase II is highly sensitive
questions.optionB: RNA polymerase III is moderately sensitive
questions.optionC: RNA polymerase I is the most sensitive polymerase
questions.optionD: Severe poisoning can cause fatal hepatic failure due to blocked mRNA synthesis
questions.explanation: Correct answer (EXCEPT): RNA polymerase I is relatively resistant, not most sensitive. Options A and B reflect standard sensitivity hierarchy. Option D is clinically correct in Amanita phalloides poisoning where mRNA synthesis failure is catastrophic in hepatocytes. Differentiating clue: Pol II is the key high-sensitivity target.

questions.questionText: A pathogenic variant in a gene's 3' UTR disrupts a miRNA binding site. The most likely molecular effect is:
questions.optionD: Reduced post-transcriptional repression with increased target protein expression
questions.explanation: Correct answer: many miRNAs repress translation or promote decay via 3' UTR binding; loss of binding can upregulate protein. Option A is replication-specific and unrelated. Option B is 5' end processing, not miRNA action. Option C is spliceosome mechanism. Clinical relevance: 3' UTR variants can be disease-causing despite unchanged coding sequence.

questions.questionText: High fidelity in translation is critically enforced at the step of:
questions.optionB: Aminoacyl-tRNA synthetase charging with editing/proofreading of misactivated amino acids
questions.optionC: Stop codon recognition by release factors only
questions.explanation: Correct answer: aminoacyl-tRNA synthetases perform key identity checks and editing, preventing wrong amino acid charging. Option A contributes catalysis but not primary amino acid identity control. Option C governs termination accuracy, not broad coding fidelity. Option D is RNA processing, not codon-amino acid matching.

questions.questionText: Which antibiotic-target pair is correctly matched for translational inhibition?
questions.optionA: Tetracycline - blocks aminoacyl-tRNA entry at 30S A site in bacteria
questions.optionB: Chloramphenicol - inhibits eukaryotic cytosolic peptidyl transferase
questions.optionC: Cycloheximide - preferred inhibitor of bacterial 30S ribosome
questions.optionD: Macrolides - block DNA gyrase in prokaryotes
questions.explanation: Correct answer: tetracyclines inhibit bacterial 30S A-site tRNA entry. Option B is wrong because chloramphenicol targets bacterial 50S peptidyl transferase (mitochondrial toxicity possible), not eukaryotic cytosolic ribosome. Option C is reversed; cycloheximide inhibits eukaryotic 60S. Option D confuses macrolides (50S translocation block) with quinolone topoisomerase targeting.

questions.questionText: A glioma with MGMT promoter methylation is more sensitive to alkylating chemotherapy mainly because:
questions.optionC: Direct repair of O6-methylguanine lesions is reduced
questions.explanation: Correct answer: MGMT mediates direct reversal of O6-alkylguanine; promoter methylation silences MGMT and increases alkylator sensitivity. Option A is not the defining mechanism here. Option B does not remove O6-methylguanine directly. Option D would generally promote repair, not sensitivity. Clinical clue: MGMT methylation predicts better response to temozolomide.

questions.questionText: Tumors with BRCA1/2 loss are particularly vulnerable to PARP inhibitors due to:
questions.optionB: Suppressed apoptosis independent of DNA repair
questions.optionD: Synthetic lethality between defective homologous recombination and blocked single-strand break repair
questions.explanation: Correct answer: BRCA-deficient tumors lack effective homologous recombination; PARP inhibition converts unrepaired single-strand lesions into lethal double-strand breaks, causing synthetic lethality. Option A is not the key vulnerability. Option B is nonspecific. Option C may occur but does not explain selective PARP sensitivity. Clinical significance: cornerstone of targeted therapy in BRCA-mutant cancers.
questions.conceptTag: BRCA homologous recombination PARP synthetic lethality

questions.questionText: Which disorder-pathway pair is correctly matched?
questions.optionA: Bloom syndrome - nucleotide excision repair failure
questions.optionB: Ataxia telangiectasia - ATM signaling defect in DNA double-strand break response
questions.optionD: Lynch syndrome - base excision repair defect
questions.explanation: Correct answer: ataxia telangiectasia involves ATM kinase dysfunction and impaired DNA double-strand break signaling. Option A is wrong because Bloom primarily involves BLM helicase and genomic instability with excess sister chromatid exchange. Option C is wrong; Fanconi pathway handles DNA interstrand crosslink biology. Option D is wrong because Lynch is mismatch repair deficiency.
questions.conceptTag: Fanconi Bloom ATM Lynch repair comparison

questions.questionText: Which statement best integrates imprinting and X-inactivation?
questions.optionA: Imprinting is parent-of-origin-specific monoallelic expression, whereas X-inactivation is dosage compensation through epigenetic silencing of one X chromosome
questions.optionB: Both mechanisms are DNA sequence mutations in coding exons
questions.optionC: Prader-Willi and Angelman syndromes result from random X-inactivation errors
questions.explanation: Correct answer: imprinting depends on parental origin marks; X-inactivation balances X gene dosage in female somatic cells. Option B confuses epigenetic regulation with sequence mutation. Option C is incorrect because Prader-Willi/Angelman relate to imprinting at chromosome 15q11-q13. Option D is false because classical X-inactivation is a female process.

questions.questionText: For rapid detection of a known microdeletion syndrome in interphase nuclei without amplification, the most suitable test is:
questions.explanation: Correct answer: FISH directly visualizes specific chromosomal loci and is highly useful for microdeletions/translocations in metaphase or interphase cells. Option A is amplification-based and may miss structural context. Option B detects protein, not DNA copy status. Option D measures RNA. Diagnostic clue: cytogenetic localization versus sequence amplification.

questions.questionText: In oncology liquid biopsy, which feature most strongly differentiates digital PCR from standard qPCR?
questions.optionA: Digital PCR cannot quantify nucleic acids
questions.optionB: Digital PCR partitions sample into many microreactions enabling absolute quantification and superior low-frequency variant detection
questions.optionC: qPCR provides absolute counts without calibration
questions.optionD: Digital PCR requires Southern blot transfer step
questions.explanation: Correct answer: digital PCR uses partitioning with endpoint counting (positive/negative partitions) for absolute quantification and high sensitivity for rare alleles. Option A is opposite. Option C is wrong because qPCR is generally relative unless standards are used. Option D incorrectly mixes unrelated technologies. Clinical value: MRD and low-level mutation tracking.
questions.conceptTag: Digital PCR versus qPCR rare variant

questions.questionText: A gene therapy strategy needs long-term expression in dividing cells for severe monogenic disease. Which vector choice is most appropriate?
questions.optionA: Naked plasmid DNA because it integrates efficiently in all mitotic cells
questions.optionB: AAV because it routinely integrates and is ideal for rapidly dividing targets
questions.optionC: Adenovirus because it establishes stable genomic integration
questions.optionD: Lentiviral vector because stable genomic integration supports durable expression in dividing lineages
questions.explanation: Correct answer: lentiviral vectors integrate into host genome, enabling durable expression across cell divisions. Option A has low in vivo integration efficiency. Option B is usually largely episomal and favored for many non-dividing targets with limited cargo. Option C is typically non-integrating and more transient. Clinical trap: AAV versus lentivirus depends on persistence, target cell turnover, and insertional risk.
questions.conceptTag: AAV versus lentiviral gene therapy vector

questions.questionText: A researcher measures absorbance at 260 nm while heating DNA. The sudden rise in absorbance is best explained by:
questions.optionA: Hyperchromic effect during strand denaturation with disruption of base stacking
questions.explanation: Correct answer: denaturation disrupts base stacking/hydrogen bonding and increases UV absorbance (hyperchromicity). Option B concerns proteins, not nucleic acid duplex transitions. Option C is not the mechanism of thermal DNA melting. Option D is opposite to strand separation. Key clue: Tm and UV profile are directly linked to duplex stability.

questions.questionText: Which statement best distinguishes homologous recombination (HR) from non-homologous end joining (NHEJ) in double-strand break repair?
questions.optionA: NHEJ requires sister chromatid template and is therefore high fidelity
questions.optionB: HR is template-independent and error-prone in G1 phase
questions.optionC: HR uses homologous template (typically S/G2) and is more accurate, whereas NHEJ directly ligates broken ends with higher mutation risk
questions.optionD: Both pathways are mechanistically identical and differ only in speed
questions.explanation: Correct answer: HR is template-guided and relatively accurate, while NHEJ is template-independent and can introduce indels. Option A reverses pathway properties. Option B is incorrect because template dependence is central to HR. Option D is false because the pathway machineries and outcomes differ substantially. Clinical relevance: BRCA defects compromise HR and alter therapy response.

questions.questionText: A child develops severe combined immunodeficiency after ADA gene transfer into hematopoietic stem cells using an integrating viral vector. This laboratory delivery method is best termed:
questions.explanation: Correct answer: vector-mediated gene delivery via virus is transduction. Transformation refers classically to uptake of naked DNA (especially in bacteria). Option C describes a non-viral physical method, not the viral process in stem-cell protocols. Option D is bacterial plasmid transfer via cell contact. Key trap: transformation versus transfection versus transduction terminology.

questions.questionText: A point mutation converts a codon for glutamate into a stop codon in the middle of an exon. This is classified as:
questions.explanation: Correct answer: conversion to premature termination codon is a nonsense mutation, often causing truncated protein or nonsense-mediated decay. Option B preserves amino acid identity, so it is incorrect here. Option C usually requires insertion/deletion not in multiples of three. Option D changes length without frameshift but does not create immediate stop by definition.
questions.conceptTag: Mutation type missense nonsense frameshift trap

questions.questionText: Which sequence is most appropriate for classic recombinant plasmid cloning workflow?
questions.optionA: Ligase treatment of vector alone, then restriction digestion, then transformation
questions.optionB: PCR amplification, direct protein translation, then plasmid methylation
questions.optionC: Transformation first, then restriction digestion inside host, then probe hybridization
questions.optionD: Restriction digestion of insert/vector, ligation, transformation into competent bacteria, selection using antibiotic marker
questions.explanation: Correct answer: standard cloning sequence is digest, ligate, transform, then select/screen colonies via selectable markers/reporter strategy. Option A misorders critical steps. Option B is unrelated to DNA assembly and bacterial selection workflow. Option C incorrectly places transformation before construct formation. Exam clue: selectable marker logic follows transformation.
questions.conceptTag: Recombinant cloning vector selectable marker workflow

questions.questionText: A forensic lab compares variable tandem-repeat regions after restriction digestion and gel separation to establish identity. The core molecular principle is:
questions.optionB: RNA editing at ADAR target sites
questions.optionC: DNA fingerprinting by polymorphic fragment-length pattern analysis
questions.explanation: Correct answer: DNA fingerprinting leverages polymorphic repeat loci and fragment-size variation (historically RFLP/VNTR, now STR-based formats). Option A is protein-based, not genomic identity testing. Option B concerns post-transcriptional modification, not forensic genotyping. Option D is unrelated therapeutics. Key clue: fragment-length polymorphism and identity probability.

questions.questionText: Compared with DNA plasmid vaccines, mRNA vaccines are often favored for rapid outbreak response mainly because:
questions.optionA: They require nuclear entry and genomic integration to express antigen
questions.optionB: They are translated in cytoplasm without need for host genomic integration, enabling fast platform redesign
questions.optionC: They are dependent on reverse transcriptase in host cells
questions.optionD: They cannot induce antigen presentation via MHC pathways
questions.explanation: Correct answer: mRNA platforms are rapidly redesignable and function via cytoplasmic translation without requiring genomic integration. Option A is wrong and conflates mechanisms. Option C is incorrect because routine mRNA vaccine action does not require endogenous reverse transcription. Option D is false since antigen expression drives immune presentation pathways. Clinical integration: platform agility was central in pandemic deployment.

questions.questionText: The end-replication problem in linear chromosomes arises because:
questions.optionA: DNA polymerase cannot synthesize DNA in 5' to 3' direction
questions.optionB: Leading strand is synthesized discontinuously and loses terminal sequence
questions.optionC: RNA polymerase cannot remove RNA primers
questions.optionD: After removal of the terminal RNA primer on lagging strand, no upstream 3' OH is available for DNA polymerase to fill the extreme 5' end
questions.explanation: Correct answer: terminal lagging-strand primer removal leaves a gap that conventional DNA polymerase cannot fill because it lacks a primer 3' OH upstream. Option A is false directionality. Option B reverses strand logic. Option C misassigns primer-removal role and misses core geometric constraint. Telomerase solves this by extending 3' overhang using RNA template.
